description: Combating Weapons of Mass Destruction training covering CBRN types, proliferation threats, counterproliferation, consequence management, detection methods, treaty compliance (NPT, CWC, BWC), and WMD response procedures. Based on JP 3-40.

questions.q: What does CBRN stand for?
questions.a: Chemical, Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear — the four categories of Weapons of Mass Destruction (WMD) threats.

questions.q: What is the definition of a Weapon of Mass Destruction (WMD)?
questions.a: A WMD is a weapon capable of a high order of destruction or causing mass casualties, including chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear weapons. Some definitions also include high-yield explosives.

questions.q: What joint publication provides doctrine for Combating Weapons of Mass Destruction?
questions.a: JP 3-40, Combating Weapons of Mass Destruction, provides joint doctrine for planning and executing CWMD operations.

questions.q: What are the three pillars of the CWMD framework?
questions.a: Nonproliferation (preventing spread), Counterproliferation (defeating WMD threats), and Consequence Management (responding to WMD incidents and minimizing effects).

questions.q: What is nonproliferation?
questions.a: Nonproliferation encompasses actions to prevent the acquisition of WMD, delivery systems, related materials, technologies, and expertise by state and non-state actors through diplomatic, legal, economic, and security means.

questions.q: What is counterproliferation?
questions.a: Counterproliferation consists of actions taken to defeat the threat and/or use of WMD against the United States, allies, and partners. It includes interdiction, deterrence, active defense, and offensive operations.

questions.q: What is consequence management in the CWMD context?
questions.a: Consequence management involves actions taken to plan for, respond to, and mitigate the effects of a WMD attack or incident, including medical response, decontamination, and restoration of essential services.

questions.q: What is the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT)?
questions.a: The NPT (1968) is an international treaty to prevent the spread of nuclear weapons, promote disarmament, and facilitate peaceful nuclear energy use. It recognizes five nuclear-weapon states (US, Russia, UK, France, China).

questions.q: Which countries have not signed the NPT?
questions.a: India, Pakistan, and Israel have never signed the NPT. North Korea withdrew in 2003. South Sudan has not acceded. India and Pakistan have confirmed nuclear arsenals; Israel maintains a policy of ambiguity.

questions.q: What is the Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC)?
questions.a: The CWC (1993) prohibits the development, production, stockpiling, and use of chemical weapons. It is administered by the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) and has near-universal membership.

questions.q: What is the Biological Weapons Convention (BWC)?
questions.a: The BWC (1972) prohibits the development, production, and stockpiling of biological and toxin weapons. Unlike the CWC, it lacks a formal verification regime, which is considered a significant weakness.

questions.q: Name the four categories of chemical warfare agents with an example of each.
questions.a: Nerve agents (Sarin/VX), Blister agents (Mustard gas), Blood agents (Hydrogen cyanide), and Choking/Pulmonary agents (Phosgene/Chlorine).

questions.q: What are nerve agents and how do they work?
questions.a: Nerve agents (Sarin, VX, Tabun, Soman, Novichok) inhibit acetylcholinesterase, causing overstimulation of muscles and glands. Symptoms include miosis (pinpoint pupils), salivation, seizures, respiratory failure, and death.

questions.q: What is the immediate treatment for nerve agent exposure?
questions.a: Administer atropine and pralidoxime (2-PAM) via auto-injector (ATNAA/NAAK), decontaminate the casualty, ensure an open airway, and provide supportive care. Self-aid and buddy-aid must be performed rapidly.

questions.q: What are biological warfare agents and how are they categorized?
questions.a: Biological agents are living organisms or toxins used to cause illness or death. CDC categorizes them: Category A (highest priority — anthrax, smallpox, plague, botulism), Category B (moderate — Q fever, brucellosis), and Category C (emerging pathogens).

questions.q: What makes biological weapons particularly challenging to detect?
questions.a: Biological agents are invisible, odorless, tasteless, and have incubation periods (hours to days). Symptoms mimic natural disease, making attribution difficult. They can be disseminated covertly through aerosols, water, or food.

questions.q: What is the difference between a radiological and a nuclear weapon?
questions.a: A nuclear weapon produces energy through fission or fusion reactions with massive blast, heat, and radiation. A radiological weapon (dirty bomb) uses conventional explosives to disperse radioactive material — no nuclear detonation occurs.

questions.q: What are the effects of a nuclear detonation?
questions.a: A nuclear detonation produces five effects: blast/shock wave (most casualties), thermal radiation (burns/fires), nuclear radiation (initial), electromagnetic pulse (EMP, disrupts electronics), and residual radiation (fallout).

questions.q: What is a 'dirty bomb' (Radiological Dispersal Device/RDD)?
questions.a: An RDD uses conventional explosives to spread radioactive material, contaminating an area. While unlikely to cause mass casualties from radiation, it creates panic, economic disruption, and costly decontamination requirements.

questions.q: What are the three principles of radiation protection?
questions.a: Time (minimize exposure duration), Distance (maximize distance from the source — inverse square law), and Shielding (use dense materials like lead, concrete, or earth between you and the source).

questions.q: What is the Proliferation Security Initiative (PSI)?
questions.a: PSI is a global initiative (2003) involving over 100 nations cooperating to interdict WMD, delivery systems, and related materials in transit by air, land, or sea to prevent proliferation by state and non-state actors.

questions.q: What role does the Defense Threat Reduction Agency (DTRA) play in CWMD?
questions.a: DTRA is the DoD combat support agency responsible for countering WMD threats. It provides technical expertise, threat reduction programs, detection capabilities, and consequence management support.

questions.q: What is the Cooperative Threat Reduction (CTR) program?
questions.a: Also known as the Nunn-Lugar program, CTR works with former Soviet states and other countries to secure and dismantle WMD, related materials, and delivery systems to prevent proliferation.

questions.q: What are WMD delivery systems?
questions.a: Delivery systems transport WMD to targets, including ballistic missiles (ICBMs, SLBMs), cruise missiles, aircraft, artillery, covert placement, aerosol sprayers, and improvised delivery methods by non-state actors.

questions.q: What is the difference between decontamination types: immediate, operational, and thorough?
questions.a: Immediate decon minimizes casualties within minutes (skin decon, RSDL). Operational decon sustains operations with limited resources (MOPP gear exchange, vehicle spray-down). Thorough decon restores full capability (detailed equipment/personnel decon).

questions.q: What is MOPP and what are the MOPP levels?
questions.a: Mission Oriented Protective Posture — levels of CBRN protective gear. MOPP 0: gear available. MOPP 1: overgarment worn. MOPP 2: add overboots. MOPP 3: add mask/hood. MOPP 4: add gloves (full protection).

questions.q: What detection equipment is used for chemical agents?
questions.a: M8/M9 detection paper (liquid agents), M256 detection kit (vapor agents), JCAD/Joint Chemical Agent Detector (automatic vapor detection), and AP2C (flame photometry detector). Each has different capabilities and limitations.

questions.q: What detection equipment is used for radiological/nuclear threats?
questions.a: AN/PDR-77 radiac meter, AN/VDR-2 (vehicle-mounted), personal dosimeters (DT-236), and advanced systems like JSLSCAD. Portal monitors and handheld detectors are used for screening cargo and personnel.

questions.q: What is the WMD-Civil Support Team (CST)?
questions.a: National Guard WMD-CSTs are 22-member full-time teams in each state/territory that support civil authorities by identifying CBRN agents/substances, assessing consequences, and advising on response measures.

questions.q: What are indicators of potential WMD use by a terrorist organization?
questions.a: Acquisition of precursor chemicals/biological agents, recruitment of scientists, unusual financial transactions related to materials, reconnaissance of critical infrastructure, and intelligence indicating intent to cause mass casualties.

questions.q: What is 'dual-use' technology in the WMD context?
questions.a: Dual-use technology has both legitimate civilian and potential WMD applications. Examples include centrifuge equipment (nuclear), fermenters (biological), and certain chemical precursors. Export controls regulate these items.

questions.q: What is the Australia Group?
questions.a: The Australia Group is an informal arrangement of 42 countries plus the EU that coordinates export controls on materials that could contribute to chemical and biological weapons programs.

questions.q: What is the Missile Technology Control Regime (MTCR)?
questions.a: The MTCR is a voluntary partnership of 35 countries that restricts exports of missiles and related technology capable of delivering WMD, specifically those with payloads over 500 kg and ranges over 300 km.

questions.q: What are the key response actions in the first hour after a suspected WMD incident?
questions.a: Establish a cordon/hot zone, initiate shelter-in-place or evacuation, don appropriate PPE, begin triage and immediate decon of casualties, identify the agent if possible, request specialized CBRN response assets, and report through chain of command.

questions.q: What is the Nuclear Suppliers Group (NSG)?
questions.a: The NSG is a 48-nation group that coordinates export controls on nuclear and nuclear-related materials, equipment, and technology to prevent nuclear weapons proliferation while allowing peaceful nuclear use.

questions.q: What is the 'comprehensive approach' to CWMD?
questions.a: The comprehensive approach integrates all instruments of national power — diplomatic, informational, military, economic, financial, intelligence, and law enforcement — across the full spectrum of CWMD activities rather than relying solely on military action.
